div.head: 209. Memorandum From Lay to the NSCSource: Transmits for comment OCDM memorandum on paragraph 60 of NSC 5906 on strategic stockpiling. Secret. 2 pp. NARA, RG 59, S/P–NSC Files: Lot 62 D 1.

list.head: REFERENCES
list.item: C. Memo for NSC from Executive Secretary, same subject, dated October 8, 1959

div.p: The enclosed memorandum by the Director, Office of Civil and Defense Mobilization, is transmitted herewith for consideration by the National Security Council of the proposed revision of paragraph 60 (Strategic Stockpiling) of NSC 5906/1 prepared on the basis of the “Proposed Policy on Strategic Materials” approved by the President in the Cabinet Meeting of September 11, 1959. The enclosed revision is a substitution for that transmitted by Reference C, prepared in the light of comments by the NSC Planning Board.

It is requested that each member of the National Security Council, the Secretary of the Treasury, the Attorney General, the Secretary of Commerce, the Director, Bureau of the Budget, and the Chairman, Atomic Energy Commission, indicate his action with respect to the enclosed proposed revision of paragraph 60 of NSC 5906/1, by completing and returning the enclosed memorandum form.Memorandum form not enclosed with this copy. [Footnote is in the original.]

postscript.p: cc: The Secretary of the Treasury
The Director, Bureau of the Budget
The Chairman, Joint Chiefs of Staff

frus:attachment.head: Memorandum From Hoegh to Lay
frus:attachment.p:
The following language is recommended for inclusion as Paragraph 60 of the Basic National Security Policy:
A stockpile of strategic and critical materials as authorized under P.L. 520, 79th Congress, should be maintained. Objectives for the strategic stockpile should be determined on the basis of the time required for supplies of materials in a national emergency to match essential needs of the emergency. Pending a determination of the essential needs of the nation after a nuclear attack (including reconstruction), the planning period should be limited to a maximum of three years, provided that until such determination is made the “maximum objective should not be less than six months usage by the U.S. industry in periods of active demand.
The suggested language is in accord with the policy on strategic materials approved by the President following the Cabinet meeting of September 11, 1959.
